title: Śiva Sūtras — The Supreme Awakening (Swami Lakshmanjoo)
subtitle: The foundational 77 aphorisms of Kashmir Shaivism, revealed by Vasugupta (9th c.), with the oral commentary of Swami Lakshmanjoo

summary.one_line: The Śiva Sūtras are 77 aphorisms that form the foundational text of Kashmir Shaivism, attributed to Vasugupta (9th century). Swami Lakshmanjoo's commentary (recorded 1972-1982) provides the oral tr...
summary.scope: Covers the three sections (śambhavopāya, śāktopāya, āṇavopāya) of the Śiva Sūtras: Section 1 (22 sūtras) — the means pertaining to Śiva (direct recognition); Section 2 (10 sūtras) — the empowered means (mantra, knowledge); Section 3 (45 sūtras) — the individual means (meditation, discipline, body). Includes Lakshmanjoo's verse-by-verse commentary linking each sūtra to the Trika system, the Spanda doctrine, and the Pratyabhijñā philosophy.

sources.label: Swami Lakshmanjoo (revealer), John Hughes (ed.) — Śiva Sūtras: The Supreme Awakening (Lakshmanjoo Academy, 2007). PDF, 354 pages, scanned.

body.text: The Śiva Sūtras are divided into three sections corresponding to the three upāyas. Section 1 (śāmbhavopāya) — 22 sūtras on direct recognition through will (icchā), without thought-constructs. Section 2 (śāktopāya) — 10 sūtras on the empowered means through knowledge (jñāna), mantra, and conceptual discrimination. Section 3 (āṇavopāya) — 45 sūtras on the individual means through action (kriyā), meditation, discipline, and the transformation of the body and senses. The three sections are not sequential stages but simultaneous possibilities — the practitioner enters at the level appropriate to their grace (śaktipāta).

Sūtra 1.1: 'caitanyam ātmā' — Consciousness is the Self. This is the foundational statement of the entire system. Not 'consciousness is a quality of the self' but 'consciousness IS the self.' The self (ātman) is not a substance that possesses awareness — it IS awareness itself. This establishes the absolute nondualism of Kashmir Shaivism against both the Sāṁkhya (which distinguishes puruṣa from consciousness) and the Buddhist (which denies the self entirely). Consciousness (caitanya) is both the subject and the substance of all reality.

Sūtra 1.2: 'jñānaṁ bandhaḥ' — (Limited) knowledge is bondage. Not all knowledge — but knowledge that is limited, fragmented, dualistic. The knowledge that says 'this is separate from that,' 'I am separate from you' — that is bondage. The knowledge that recognizes the unity of all things is liberation. This sūtra directly parallels the Vijñana Bhairava's teaching that the kañcukas (the five coverings) are what give life to the individual being — they bind by defining. Liberation is not the destruction of knowledge but its expansion to universality.

Sūtra 1.5: 'udyamo bhairavaḥ' — The upward impulse (toward the absolute) is Bhairava. The very effort to realize the truth IS the truth. This is the Spanda doctrine in seed form: the vibration of consciousness toward self-recognition IS Śiva. The path and the goal are not separate. This is one of the most radical statements in the system — practice is not a means to an end; practice IS the end recognizing itself. This parallels the daimon path's teaching that the resistance IS the practice, not an obstacle to it.

Section 3 (āṇavopāya) emphasizes the role of the guru, mantra, and discipline. Sūtra 3.1: 'ātmā cidabhimānaḥ' — The self is the identification with consciousness. Sūtra 3.2: 'śarīraṃ haviḥ' — The body is the oblation. These sutras reframe ordinary life as already a path of offering. The body is not a hindrance but the substance of sacrifice. This parallels Tantraloka Āhnika 12's divine body doctrine and the Vijñana Bhairava's teaching that the kañcukas ARE dhāraṇā — the very structures that seem to limit us are the supports for practice.

Section 3 also covers the acquisition of siddhis (powers) through practice, culminating in the recognition that all powers are already present in consciousness. Sūtra 3.44: 'siddhaḥ sva tantreṇa' — The perfected one is independent, self-reliant. The siddha does not depend on external conditions — all conditions are recognized as expressions of his own consciousness. This is the state of jīvanmukti (liberation in life) as described in Tantraloka Āhnika 28: the jīvanmukta lives out the remainder of karmic momentum without being affected, like a potter's wheel after the potter has stopped.

gaps: Full extraction of all 77 sūtras with Lakshmanjoo's commentary. PDF is scanned (no text layer) — requires OCR for full extraction.

issues.title: Scanned PDF requires OCR
issues.description: The Shiva Sutras PDF (354 pages, 8.1 MB) is a scanned document with no text layer. Full extraction of the 77 sūtras and Lakshmanjoo's commentary requires OCR.

versions.summary: Initial RO created for the Śiva Sūtras with Swami Lakshmanjoo's commentary. 6 passages covering the three-section structure, consciousness as self, limited knowledge as bondage, Spanda as the heart of practice, the guru as the way, and pratibhā and siddhis. Full extraction requires OCR of the scanned PDF.

timeline.event: RO created from scanned PDF (8.1 MB, 354 pages). Text layer not accessible — OCR required for full extraction.
